title: The promise you keep without telling anyone
summary: Describe a private promise or commitment you made to yourself that no one else knows about, and reflect on why you keep it.
topic: The promise you keep without telling anyone
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a private promise or personal rule you follow that you have never explained to anyone, and why keeping it matters to you. Use at least one relative clause, a conditional sentence, the present perfect for reflection, and 'however' or 'although' for concession.
hintStarters: There is something I do every time a certain situation comes up, and I have never once explained it to anyone who has noticed…
I made a decision years ago — not the kind you announce, but the kind you simply start living by…
If anyone ever asked me why I do this, I am not sure I could give a complete answer, but I know that stopping would feel like…

hintVocab.meaningVi: quy tắc riêng tư, nguyên tắc cá nhân
tự chịu trách nhiệm với bản thân
không được chú ý, không ai biết
cảm thấy bị thôi thúc phải làm gì
la bàn đạo đức, cảm giác về điều đúng sai
sống đúng với, đáp ứng kỳ vọng

sampleText:
Years ago, after watching a close friend lose a business because a partner had taken credit for work that was not his, I made a decision that I have never explained to anyone: I would never put my name on something I did not substantially contribute to. It sounds simple, and perhaps obvious. But the situations in which this rule gets tested are rarely obvious. A group report where your section was weak; a project that went well mainly because of one colleague; a presentation where someone else's idea became the centrepiece. In those moments the easy path is to stay quiet and accept the credit that comes your way. I have chosen, every time, not to do that.

Although nobody has ever asked me directly why I redistribute credit the way I do — by mentioning names, pointing to contributions, saying 'this was mostly her thinking' — a few people have noticed. A manager in Nairobi once asked me, in a tone that was half curious and half suspicious, whether I was trying to make a point. I told her I simply wanted to be accurate. That was true, but it was not the full truth. The full truth is that the rule I follow is a promise I made to myself after watching what dishonesty about credit does to people over time. It does not only damage the person who was cheated. It also does something to the person who took what was not theirs — a slow erosion of whatever made them trustworthy in the first place.

What I have found, after many years of keeping this promise quietly, is that it does not go entirely unnoticed — it simply goes unannounced. People notice patterns even when they cannot name them. A colleague in Seoul told me once that she trusted me in a specific way she could not quite articulate. If I had broken my own rule even once, visibly and for clear personal gain, I suspect that unnameable trust would have started to erode. The value of a private promise is not that it makes you look good. It is that it keeps your behaviour consistent whether or not you are being observed. That consistency, I have come to believe, is the foundation of every kind of integrity that actually means something.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional for imagined consequence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If I had broken my own rule even once, visibly and for clear personal gain, I suspect that unnameable trust would have started to erode' dùng 'had + past participle' trong mệnh đề 'if' và 'would have + past participle' trong mệnh đề chính — điều kiện loại 3, diễn đạt điều có thể đã xảy ra nếu hành vi trong quá khứ khác đi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If I had broken my own rule even once, visibly and for clear personal gain, I suspect that unnameable trust would have started to erode' uses 'had + past participle' in the if-clause and 'would have + past participle' in the main clause — a third conditional expressing what might have happened if past behaviour had been different.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for accumulated experience
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What I have found, after many years of keeping this promise quietly' và 'I have come to believe' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn đạt sự nhận thức hoặc kinh nghiệm tích lũy qua một khoảng thời gian dài — không phải một phát hiện đơn lẻ mà là kết quả của nhiều năm quan sát.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What I have found, after many years of keeping this promise quietly' and 'I have come to believe' use the present perfect to express understanding or experience accumulated over a long period — not a single discovery but the result of years of observation.

sampleTranslationVi:
Nhiều năm trước, sau khi chứng kiến một người bạn thân mất đi một doanh nghiệp vì một đối tác đã nhận công lao của công việc không phải của mình, tôi đã đưa ra quyết định mà tôi chưa bao giờ giải thích cho ai: tôi sẽ không bao giờ đặt tên mình lên điều gì mà tôi không đóng góp đáng kể. Nghe có vẻ đơn giản, và có lẽ hiển nhiên. Nhưng những tình huống mà quy tắc này bị thử thách hiếm khi rõ ràng. Một bản báo cáo nhóm mà phần của bạn còn yếu; một dự án thành công chủ yếu nhờ một đồng nghiệp; một bài thuyết trình mà ý tưởng của người khác trở thành trọng tâm. Trong những khoảnh khắc đó, con đường dễ dàng là giữ im lặng và chấp nhận công nhận đến với bạn. Tôi đã chọn, mỗi lần, không làm điều đó.

Mặc dù không ai bao giờ hỏi tôi trực tiếp tại sao tôi phân phối lại sự ghi nhận theo cách tôi làm — bằng cách đề cập đến tên, chỉ ra đóng góp, nói 'đây chủ yếu là suy nghĩ của cô ấy' — một số người đã nhận thấy. Một người quản lý ở Nairobi một lần hỏi tôi, với giọng điệu vừa tò mò vừa nghi ngờ, liệu tôi có đang cố tình thể hiện điều gì không. Tôi nói với cô ấy rằng tôi chỉ muốn chính xác. Điều đó đúng, nhưng đó không phải là sự thật đầy đủ. Sự thật đầy đủ là quy tắc tôi theo là một lời hứa tôi đã tự đưa ra sau khi chứng kiến sự không trung thực về việc nhận công làm gì với mọi người theo thời gian. Nó không chỉ gây hại cho người bị lừa dối. Nó cũng làm điều gì đó với người đã lấy những gì không phải của mình — sự xói mòn chậm chạp của bất cứ điều gì đã làm họ đáng tin ngay từ đầu.

Điều tôi nhận ra, sau nhiều năm giữ lời hứa này trong im lặng, là nó không hoàn toàn không được chú ý — nó chỉ đơn giản là không được thông báo. Mọi người nhận ra những mô hình dù họ không thể đặt tên cho chúng. Một đồng nghiệp ở Seoul một lần nói với tôi rằng cô ấy tin tưởng tôi theo một cách cụ thể mà cô ấy không thể diễn đạt rõ ràng. Nếu tôi đã vi phạm quy tắc của chính mình dù chỉ một lần, một cách rõ ràng và vì lợi ích cá nhân rõ ràng, tôi nghi ngờ rằng niềm tin không thể đặt tên đó đã bắt đầu xói mòn. Giá trị của một lời hứa riêng tư không phải là nó làm cho bạn trông tốt. Đó là nó giữ cho hành vi của bạn nhất quán dù bạn có đang bị quan sát hay không. Sự nhất quán đó, tôi dần nhận ra và tin, là nền tảng của mọi loại chính trực thực sự có ý nghĩa.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly?
options: The situations in that this rule gets tested are rarely obvious.
The situations which this rule gets tested in them are rarely obvious.
The situations in which this rule gets tested are rarely obvious.
The situations in which this rule gets tested it are rarely obvious.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In which' is the correct formal relative structure when the relative clause requires a preposition before 'which'; no pronoun repetition is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: After a preposition, use 'which' (for things), not 'that'; do not repeat the noun with a pronoun.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly forms a third conditional?
options: If I broke my own rule even once, that trust would have started to erode.
If I had broken my own rule even once, that trust would start to erode.
If I had broken my own rule even once, that trust will have started to erode.
If I had broken my own rule even once, that trust would have started to erode.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: 'had + past participle' in the if-clause and 'would have + past participle' in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect in the if-clause; 'would have + past participle' in the main clause.

options: Although nobody has ever asked me directly, a few people have noticed.
Although nobody has ever asked me directly but a few people have noticed.
Although, nobody has ever asked me directly, a few people have noticed.
Nobody has ever asked me directly although, a few people have noticed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but' in the main clause, no comma directly after 'although'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' introduces a concessive clause; never add 'but' after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly for accumulated experience?
options: What I found, after many years of keeping this promise quietly, is that it goes unannounced.
What I have found, after many years of keeping this promise quietly, is that it goes unannounced.
What I have find, after many years of keeping this promise quietly, is that it goes unannounced.
What I have been finding, after many years of keeping this promise quietly, is that it goes unannounced.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have found' is present perfect of 'find'; 'have find' is incorrect; simple past 'found' loses the link to the present.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect 'have found' shows experience accumulated up to the present moment.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'whether or not'?
options: Your behaviour stays consistent whether or not are you being observed.
Your behaviour stays consistent if or not you are being observed.
Your behaviour stays consistent whether or not you are being observed.
Your behaviour stays consistent whether you are or not being observed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whether or not + clause' is the fixed structure meaning 'regardless of whether'; the word order after 'whether or not' is subject-verb.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whether or not' is followed by normal subject-verb order; 'if or not' is not a standard phrase.

options: It does not go entirely unnoticed — it simply goes unannouncing.
It does not go entirely unnoticed — it simply goes to unannounced.
It does not go entirely unnoticed — it simply goes unannouncedly.
It does not go entirely unnoticed — it simply goes unannounced.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Go + past participle' is the correct structure for describing a state that is not acted upon (go unnoticed, go unannounced, go unreported).
mcQuestions.hint: 'Go + past participle' (unnoticed, unannounced) describes a state that continues without someone acting on it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'hold yourself accountable'?
options: A private promise forces you to hold yourself accountable even when no one is watching.
A private promise forces you to hold yourself accountably even when no one is watching.
A private promise forces you to hold yourself to account even when no one is watching.
A private promise forces you to hold yourself accounting even when no one is watching.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Hold yourself accountable' (adjective after the verb) is the standard collocation; 'hold yourself to account' is also correct but option C replaces 'accountable' with it inconsistently.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Hold yourself accountable' uses the adjective 'accountable'; 'accountably' (adverb) is not used here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'come to believe' correctly?
options: I have come believing that consistency is the foundation of integrity.
I have come to believe that consistency is the foundation of integrity.
I have come to believed that consistency is the foundation of integrity.
I came to believe that consistency is the foundation of integrity.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have come to + base verb' expresses a gradual realisation still relevant now; 'have come to believed' is incorrect because 'to' requires the base form.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Have come to + base verb' — use the base form after 'to', not the past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'put one's name on'?
options: I would never put my name at work I did not substantially contribute to.
I would never put my name in work I did not substantially contribute to.
I would never put my name on work I did not substantially contribute to.
I would never put my name over work I did not substantially contribute to.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Put your name on something' is the correct collocation meaning to take credit for or sign something; 'at', 'in', and 'over' are all incorrect.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Put your name on something' = to claim credit for it; the preposition is always 'on'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'live up to' correctly?
options: It is hard to live to up a promise you made to yourself years ago.
It is hard to live up a promise you made to yourself years ago.
It is hard to live up for a promise you made to yourself years ago.
It is hard to live up to a promise you made to yourself years ago.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Live up to' is a fixed phrasal verb meaning to meet or maintain the standards of something; the full phrase is 'live up to + noun'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Live up to' = to fulfil or match expectations; 'to' is part of the phrasal verb, not interchangeable.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'resist the temptation' correctly?
options: I had to resist the temptation to stay quiet and accept credit that was not mine.
I had to resist temptation to stay quiet and accept credit that was not mine.
I had to resist the temptation of staying quiet and accept credit that was not mine.
I had to resist the temptation for staying quiet and accept credit that was not mine.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Resist the temptation to + infinitive' is the correct structure; 'the' is required before 'temptation' when it refers to a specific temptation.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Resist the temptation to + infinitive' — use 'to' before the verb, not 'of' or 'for'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If a private promise only works because you are the only one who knows about it, does telling someone else make it stronger or weaker — and what does your answer reveal about why you keep it?
